title: Malted granary loaf
subtitle: A seeded tin loaf with sweetness from the malted flour
excerpt: A 900 g malted tin loaf built on granary flour — the flecked, slightly sweet British baking flour with malted wheat grains. Mixed seeds on top, a modest 65% hydration, and a single overnight proof in the fridge. The loaf slices cleanly and keeps for four days.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the malted grain loaf is documented across the Midlands and North of England as a farmhouse staple, made with stone-ground flour that retained the bran and malted grain fragments. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the household bread chapter discusses the qualities of different British flour grades; the malted or brown flour loaf is recommended for keeping quality and digestibility. The baker's percentages here : 65% hydration, 2% salt, 1.4% yeast. These are the same straight-dough anchor as a white tin loaf; the granary flour's absorption is similar to strong white.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf, freeze in a bag. Toasts from frozen perfectly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix and shape in the evening; the tin goes straight into the fridge for an 8-hour cold proof. Bake in the morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, middle shelf; reduce to 200°C after 15 minutes
baking.steamMethod: small heatproof bowl of water on the oven floor for the first 15 minutes

recipeTools.notes: The most reliable way to know the loaf is done.

glossaryTerms.definition: Deflating a risen dough by folding or punching it down. The aim is to redistribute the yeast and even out the gas distribution before shaping. It doesn't destroy the fermentation; it reorganises it.

content.text: Granary flour is strong bread flour blended with malted wheat grains and rye flour — the British miller's answer to the wheaty, slightly sweet German-style bread that had been popular since the Victorian era. The malted grains give the loaf its characteristic speckled crumb and a quiet sweetness that plain white bread doesn't have. It keeps better than a standard white tin, slices cleanly when a day old, and makes the most dependable everyday sandwich bread.

The overnight cold-proof approach used here is the same as a white tin loaf: mix in the evening, shape into the tin, refrigerate overnight, and bake in the morning from cold. The fridge slows the yeast and lets the flavour develop while you sleep. The dough goes into the oven cold and proves as the oven heats around it — you get a good oven spring and a tight crumb that holds up to slicing.

items.prepNote: granary or malted brown bread flour — or strong wholemeal if you can't find granary
1 teaspoon; or use treacle for a darker flavour
sunflower, pumpkin, sesame — for the top

content.text: Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}} granary flour and {{wholemeal-bread-flour}} wholemeal bread flour in the large bowl. Add the {{yeast-fast-action}} fast-action yeast on one side and the {{sea-salt-fine}} fine sea salt on the other. Dissolve the {{honey}} in the {{water}} lukewarm water and add the {{vegetable-oil}}. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and mix until a rough dough forms. Turn out and knead for 10 minutes by hand, or 7 minutes in a stand mixer with a dough hook, until the dough is smooth and pulls cleanly from the surface. Granary dough feels slightly grainier than a plain white dough — the malted grains are visible in the surface; this is correct.
Cover the bowl and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes, until the dough has roughly doubled. Wholemeal flours ferment a little faster than white; in a warm kitchen this may take only 75 minutes.
Lightly oil a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin.

the dough by pressing the air out and folding it a few times. Shape it into a log the length of the tin, place seam-side down in the tin, and press it to fill the corners. If using seeds, brush the top lightly with water and scatter the {{mixed-seeds}} over the surface, pressing them in gently so they adhere.

Cover the tin loosely with cling film and refrigerate overnight. The dough should rise to the rim of the tin, or just over, by morning. If it has risen and then fallen slightly, it has over-fermented; this is rare with a modest yeast percentage but possible in a warm fridge.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Place a small heatproof bowl of water on the oven floor. Remove the cling film from the tin and place the cold loaf directly in the oven — do not let it come to room temperature first; the cold centre creates oven spring as it warms up. Bake for 15 minutes, then reduce the heat to 200°C and bake for a further 20–25 minutes, until the crust is a deep brown and the loaf sounds hollow when tapped on the base. An internal probe should read 96°C. Turn out immediately and cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before slicing.

attrs.heading: When things go wrong
attrs.intro: Two common issues with granary loaves.

items.symptom: Loaf is dense and gummy inside despite a dark crust
items.cause: Underbaked, or cut while still hot.
items.fix: The internal probe is the most reliable check; 96°C throughout. Let the loaf cool fully on a rack before slicing — a hot loaf continues to cook from residual heat and the crumb structure sets as it cools. Slicing hot compresses the crumb and produces a gummy texture.

items.cause: Surface not wet enough before the seeds were applied.
items.fix: Brush the top thoroughly with water (the surface should be visibly moist) before scattering the seeds. Press them in lightly with your palm. An egg wash gives stronger adhesion if seeds-falling-off becomes a recurring problem.
